title: Four-shaft point twill sampler
subtitle: Threading a straight draw and then a point draw on four shafts to compare the diagonal directions
excerpt: A point twill threading creates a V-shaped chevron by reversing the threading direction at the midpoint. This sampler threads half the warp on a straight draw and half on a point draw, then weaves both with the same treadling sequence to show how the threading direction controls the chevron.
sourceNotes: Point twill threading documented in Mary E. Black, The Key to Weaving (1945, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910, public domain, Internet Archive). The point threading producing a chevron is a standard four-shaft structure.

glossaryTerms.definition: A threading sequence that rises to a maximum shaft number and then reverses, creating a mirror-image pattern. On four shafts: 1-2-3-4-3-2 repeated produces the classic point-twill chevron.
A grid diagram showing which shaft each warp thread passes through. Reading the threading draft tells you the repeating order to thread the heddles.
A weave structure in which the weft passes over two or more warp threads before interlacing, advancing one thread each pick to produce a diagonal rib on the cloth surface.

content.text: chevron by reversing the threading direction at a midpoint. This sampler weaves both a straight draw and a point draw side by side from the same treadling sequence, using the
to show exactly how the threading direction controls the resulting diagonal.

items.name: Smooth wool or cotton warp yarn (8/2)

content.text: A point threading on four shafts reads 1-2-3-4-3-2 and repeats. The sequence rises from shaft 1 to shaft 4, then falls back to shaft 2, then rises again. The shared shaft 4 at the top and shaft 1 at the bottom are where the chevron points form. The number of full repeats determines how many chevron peaks appear across the cloth width.

Thread the loom following the point draft above. Check each threading repeat as you go: the heddles on shaft 1 should be flanked on each side by shaft 2, shaft 2 flanked by shaft 3, and shaft 4 at the apex. Any threading error will create a straight diagonal rather than a true chevron at that point in the cloth.

Treadle the standard 2/2 twill sequence (treadles 1-2-3-4 repeating) and weave at a balanced sett. The point threading will produce a clear V-shaped chevron in the woven cloth, with the diagonal reversing at each threading peak. Weave 15 to 20 cm to establish the pattern, then try a different treadling sequence to see how the point threading responds to different treadle orders.

items.problem: The diagonal rib does not reverse at the expected point, producing a straight line instead of a chevron
items.cause: A threading error at the reversal point: a thread that should be on shaft 1 was threaded on shaft 3, or vice versa
items.fix: Count the heddle sequence from both ends toward the reversal point. The apex should be a single shaft 4 heddle flanked on both sides by shaft 3. If both sides read as shaft 3-2-1 from the apex, the threading is correct.

items.problem: The diagonal is broken with a hole or float at the chevron peak
items.cause: A shaft 4 thread was missed in the threading and the sequence jumped from 3 to 3 at the apex
items.fix: Add the missing shaft-4 heddle thread. On a finished warp this requires inserting a floating heddle; on an incomplete thread-up, correct before sleying the reed.
